Analytics Report: Quality & Scrap Diagnostics (Metal Fabrication)

Contents
Executive Summary
Findings 1. Bending × Shift B 2. Supplier C Material 3. High Complexity Parts
Methodology
Section 1

Executive Summary

Across 9,284 production work orders and 139,483 parts inspected between January 2023 and December 2025, the overall defect rate was 6.0% and total scrap cost was $608,872. Both metrics have remained broadly steady over the historical period, with a slight upward trend.

Defect Rate by Month
Total Scrap Cost by Month

We built a data pipeline to extract, standardize, and merge records across all four systems into a single source, then conducted comprehensive analytical diagnostics to surface previously unseen patterns.

As a result of this analysis, three distinct patterns emerged that point to specific, concentrated, and addressable operational drivers of elevated quality cost. Bending operations on Shift B run at 2.1× the defect rate of Shift A; material sourced from Supplier C at 1.4× other suppliers; and high-complexity parts at 2.4× lower-complexity equivalents. None of these patterns were visible within any single source system.

The financial impact of these elevated defect rates extends well beyond the direct cost of scrapped material. Each defective run also requires rework labor, disrupts downstream scheduling when jobs must be remade, adds inspection overhead, and potentially carries customer relationship costs.

Section 2

Findings

Each finding below represents a defect rate elevation that is statistically significant, consistent across the analysis period, and only quantifiable through cross-system joins.

Bending operations on Shift B produce defects at 2.1× the rate of Shift A
2.1×
vs Bending Shift A
  • Bending × Shift B aggregate defect rate: 10.7% vs 5.1% on Shift A — a 2.1× elevation.
  • The Shift B elevation is statistically significant (Welch t-test, p < 0.05) and visible in the distribution of individual work order outcomes: the median defect rate and the spread of outcomes are both higher on Shift B.
  • The pattern is persistent across the full analysis period — not a short-term anomaly. The monthly trend chart shows Shift B running above Shift A in essentially every month of the 36-month window.
  • The elevation is specific to bending operations and does not appear on other machine types (laser cutting, punching, welding). Bending is the most operator-dependent process on the floor — small differences in setup, technique, or in-process verification produce measurable dimensional variation in ways that are less likely on CNC-driven operations.
  • Likely drivers include inconsistent equipment recalibration between shifts, setup state handoff gaps, and operator experience differentials — all of which are more consequential in bending than in other operations due to the direct role of operator judgment in achieving accurate bend angles.
Defect Rate by Machine Type × Shift
Defect Rate Distribution — Bending by Shift vs All Machines
Bending Defect Rate Over Time — Shift B vs Shift A
Supplier C material is associated with a 1.4× elevated defect rate
1.4×
vs all other suppliers
  • Supplier C aggregate defect rate: 7.7% vs 5.5% for all other suppliers — a 1.4× elevation.
  • The differential persists across machine types and shifts, indicating a material quality issue rather than a downstream process issue.
  • Critically, the elevation holds within every complexity tier: Supplier C's High-complexity defect rate is 17.5% vs 9.9% for other suppliers on the same complexity tier. The complexity mix across suppliers is broadly consistent — Supplier C does not disproportionately supply high-complexity parts — ruling out complexity as a confounding factor.
  • The monthly defect rate chart shows Supplier C running persistently above all other suppliers across the analysis period, with no convergence trend.
Defect Rate by Supplier
Complexity Mix by Supplier
Defect Rate by Complexity Tier × Supplier
Monthly Defect Rate by Supplier
High-complexity parts fail at 2.4× the rate of other complexity tiers
2.4×
vs non-High complexity
  • High-complexity aggregate defect rate: 12.0% vs 5.0% for all other tiers — the strongest single-dimension signal in the dataset.
  • Defect rate elevation is most pronounced in bending operations, running at about double the defect rate of other operations using high complexity parts.
  • The relationship is monotonic: Low → Medium → High tracks with strictly increasing defect rates across all 36 months. This is not a threshold effect — complexity elevation is gradual and consistent.
  • The complexity effect is not uniform across machine types. The grouped chart shows that certain equipment types show a more pronounced sensitivity to complexity than others, suggesting that machine capability and tooling condition interact with part complexity in producing defects.
  • The monthly trend shows the High-complexity tier running above Medium and Low in every month of the analysis period, with no sign of convergence.
Defect Rate by Complexity Tier
Defect Rate by Complexity × Machine Type
Monthly Defect Rate by Complexity
Section 3

Methodology

Data Sources Inspection records and scrap events from the QMS; production work orders and part catalog from the ERP; material lot receipts and certification status from the WMS; operator records from the HR system. The analysis covers January 2023 through December 2025 — 9,284 work orders and 139,483 parts inspected across all five production lines.
Pipeline Data was extracted from each source system and loaded into a DuckDB analytical database using dlt. Transformation and join logic was implemented in dbt, producing mart-layer tables that serve as the basis for this analysis. All logic is version-controlled and reproducible.
Defect Rate Definition Defect rate is defined as quantity failed divided by quantity inspected at the work order level. Aggregate rates are volume-weighted (total failed / total inspected across the group). Statistical significance is assessed using Welch's t-test. Effect sizes are reported as multipliers relative to the comparison group.
Known Data Limitations Approximately 606 scrap events reference inspection records removed during QMS deduplication. These are retained in cost calculations via work order association and do not affect defect rate calculations. Approximately 15% of work orders have no material lot association due to missing scan records at job start; these are excluded from supplier analyses.
